dialogues.text: But it's just like crazy. I mean, this goes into my one of my pet theories we talked about before with Lorelei, which is movies as money laundering enterprise. The marketing budget for this movie was $100 million. And they didn't know what the name of the movie was! [Haitch & Lorelei laugh]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: That is dangerous.

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: Oh, man.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Alright, let's get into, let's get into this movie. And just sort of just sort of explore what happened.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Let's explore!

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah. So it opens up with news reports, which I thought was kind of a fun, kind of like, you know, like staticky news reports coming through and some major exposition dump, in terms of what was going on.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: More CNN anchors getting to do

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: Yeah, where's my Wolf Blitzer?

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Wolf wasn't available?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: No, Wolf was there!

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Oh he's in there?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: He is in there. But he doesn't say anything.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Was he really in there?!

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yes, yes.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: They couldn't make Wolf's quote. They already spent $100 million not naming the movie, they couldn't pay Wolf his 200k.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: But one of the things about that whole prologue is that it is super efficient, it gets you, it tells a ton of story in a real short amount of time. And I totally admire that it's like okay, this is the situation, aliens attack and this is where we are! Okay, it's like boom, just get you there. Whereas entire films have, you know, spent 40 minutes on that premise previously. And just on a technical note, every, all of the, one of the things that movies don't usually get right is just mimicking like cable news stuff. And sometimes it's the performances, sometimes it's the graphics, it's how they shoot the anchors and, and how dramatic or undramatic the the performances are. I think most of the almost all of it, even though a lot of it is stock footage, cleverly edited together, it nails a certain amount of authenticity, it's like this is kind of how it would really be reported. Including Cage's appearances in those clips. Yeah. of he's the spokesperson and he's the one telling the the military's story. So I give it high marks for authenticity in that respect.

dialogues.name: HAITCH I
dialogues.text: picked up two things on my second viewing yesterday. One was that he says twice, "with very little training, you can use these suits." So I thought that was cool that they established that he could get, you know, made it realistic that he can get started. And then the second piece was, he is on the air announcing Operation Downfall, the biggest mechanized assault in history? Like I thought this was a secret? [Lorelei laughs]

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: The aliens, the aliens don't watch the news. The mimics aren't watching CNN, they don't have cable. Can we talk about the fact that like they decided that what they needed to do is land a helicopter in Trafalgar Square at the beginning of this movie?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Oh yeah. Bad ass.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: That just seems aggressive. That just, I mean, like, what's even involved in doing that? Like, how do you, where do you go for those permits?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: You didn't see that in the in the behind the scenes? They covered this.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Oh, what's the story?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: They had three hours, they had three hours and whatever they got was what they would get. And Tom, like they shot outside and then Doug got on and they flew around shooting reaction shots of Tom. And as they were filming, that's where he's like sleeping, and he wakes up right? While he was doing that somebody was right next to him throwing up. Like the entire flight.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Why?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: 'Cause helicopters, man.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: No, his performance was pretty good. It didn't, you know, no reason to throw up. [Lorelei & Haitch laugh]

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Do they say how much it costs?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: They did not say how much it costs.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: How much do you think it costs? Let's take some guesses. What would you have to pay to get Trafalgar Square for three hours?

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Great Britain loves it when movies shoot there...

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Is that true?

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Maybe it was - well, considering how much money they give to movies to shoot there. Yeah, a lot! A lot. They love those photo ops of Tom Cruise shaking hands with the Prime Minister.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Interesting.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: They're very willing to pay for it.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Alright. I want to see some invoices.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: You're really accounting focus tonight. This is like a big thing for you.

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: He wants to uncover, the you know -

dialogues.name: JASON I
dialogues.text: want to know who's getting what. Somebody's getting paid. Somebody's got an LLC that's like, you know, Trafalgar Square Enterprises LLC. And you know, they they did a handshake deal. I don't know. They get the crown jewels or something.

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: Next thing you know, Tom Cruise landed his helicopter.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah, exactly.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So we establish pretty effectively that Tom is pretty cowardly and deal making like he has tried to blackmail the general he has tried to do anything he can to try and get him out himself out of this. I love the detail that like he's like backing up as the general is approaching him and he bumps into the chair like he's really, he's really racked, but I thought Brandon Gleeson. I couldn't tell him sometimes it felt like he wasn't really paying attention and, but he was also just like a stoic force who I ultimately liked.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Father of Domhnall Gleeson.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Really?!

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Oh! Of course!

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Wow. That makes sense.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: One of the things again, with the efficiency of this film isn't I think it's like five minutes into the film when Gleeson is telling him no, you're going to the front to report on this. And in that time, the audience built up expectations of what, you know, Tom Cruise's charm. I mean, he's literally the spokesperson for the military's efforts. Big smile, super convincing. This is Tom Cruise in his element. Okay, super persuasive, and then immediately pushed back. And like, is you know, there's a certain amount of reality and like, oh, crap, my charm is run out. This is not going to work anymore. And he's, he's, he's doing, he's doing his best to get out of this. You know, and, you know, I can leverage this and then and then it eventually merges into him trying to extort and blackmail him.

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: And then just bailing. [Lorelei laughs]

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Well, you know, what, Tom Cruise, sometimes the best solution is for run, right?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: He is fast.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: But how quickly, I mean, to use that charm, and to be shown as vulnerable as he is for this next giant chunk of the movie is very, you know, un-Tom Cruise like, where, you know, he's usually chest out, he's usually the guy with the plan. And if he's reluctant, that's one thing, but he usually knows what he's doing. And for so long in this movie, that's why I loved that he kind of, it's a sort of play against type. It goes into it later in the movie when he literally builds up confidence and knowledge and strength and all of that other stuff. But I love how vulnerable he appeared in this movie. And even then, this is just a minor blocking technical thing. You guys tell me what you think. Tom Cruise is not a tall man. Okay? And I'm saying this as a not a tall man. He appears short in this movie in such a way that you don't usually see in these movies. And I think that has something I don't know if that was conscious or subconscious. He is totally out of his element. And he is very vulnerable. And I just like that's one of the things I love about this movie is that it's kind of his movie, you know, his type of movie, but also kind of not.

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: Yeah, but it also echoes to like him like old Tom Cruise, right? Like I mean, not young Tom Cruise. But like when he flashes that smile, it's like I feel like I hadn't seen like that smile in decades. Right? And he just like, he's really turning it on. And then seeing him scared was, that was pretty hilarious.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: That's what makes this movie like, I mean, someone was, we were talking to someone who's like not a Tom Cruise fan. I was like, this is a pretty good one to watch. Because like, you get to see him in this role where he's like, kind of a cowardly schmuck. And he's, you know, he gets killed a lot, which is also something you see a lot in a Tom Cruise movie. So that's great.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Yeah. If you have a problem with his like, style of acting or whatever. This is, like you said, this is your movie because he gets beat up. He gets insulted over and over and over again. [Lorelei laughs]

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Just repeatedly shot by Emily Blunt.

dialogues.name: HAITCH I
dialogues.text: do want to call up before we leave Brendan Gleeson. But the first time I saw him was in 28 Days Later. Any take on 28 Days Later, first mini DV film ever shot.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: That was Danny Boyle. Right?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah!

dialogues.name: TODD I
dialogues.text: haven't seen that in a really long time. But I really liked it. I remember it.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: The scene where he dies is super sad. I remember that. More than the rest of the movie. That's another movie that has 17 endings as well. They were just like, it just keeps on trying some stuff.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah. Alright. So he is sent on his way. He's arrested. He is stunned into unconsciousness and he wakes up on the ground and Heathrow. And we are introduced to Bill Paxton, rest in power, our man, Bill Paxton.

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: Yes!

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: But first Tony Todd! Right? That's Tony Todd.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah, of course.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Amazing Tony Todd! "Wake up maggot!"

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: What do we, what do we like Tony Todd? Oh, he's been in -

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Candyman!

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Candyman, yeah, yeah.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: And Next Generation.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: And Next Generation. Yeah. He's 6'5'', to Todd's point about blocking. He's 6'5'' and I believe that Tom Cruise is the opposite of those numbers. [Lorelei & Haitch laugh]

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: So how tall is Paxton because I was noticing that he was significantly taller than Tom as well.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Definitely played significantly taller.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah, that was wild.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: He's just six feet tall. Tom Cruise is 4'2''. [Lorelei & Todd laugh]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So we're getting all of these nice details where you know, the things that Bill Paxton is is laying out. And the way it unfolds I thought is is really great there. But we get them in into the barracks and we have the introduction of J Squad. So first of all, can we just talk about how aliens is the template for every action movie squad in history?

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: Oh yeah.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Absolutely.

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: Oh yeah.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: That's why it's so awesome that Bill Paxton is in the scene. [clip of Alien plays]

dialogues.name: HUDSON
dialogues.text: Well, that's great. That's just fucking great man! [clip of Alien ends]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah!

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Like that was genius casting. Whoever was like, I assume they looked at no one else. [Lorelei laughs]

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: God I hope not, I don't want to hear about any other potential casting.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So first of all, this is another one just like we talked about with Fallout. They had no script. They basically had storyboards and visual elements. So he said he didn't even have names for any of J Squad. He just started seeing actors. And he's like, I like you.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Griff, you're in!

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah, exactly. Exactly. I do love the fact that Paxton as he's talking them through stuff, he's saying all this foreshadowing like - [clip of Edge of Tomorrow plays]

dialogues.name: MASTER SERGEANT FARELL
dialogues.text: Private Kimmel, what is my view of gambling in the barracks?

dialogues.name: KIMMEL
dialogues.text: Dislike it, Sergeant Farell.

dialogues.name: MASTER SERGEANT FARELL
dialogues.text: Vance, why do I dislike it?

dialogues.name: VANCE
dialogues.text: The entire notion that our fate is in hands other than our own.

dialogues.name: MASTER SERGEANT FARELL
dialogues.text: And what is my definitive position on the concept of fate chorus?

dialogues.name: SQUAD
dialogues.text: Through readiness and discipline we are masters of our fate.

dialogues.name: MASTER SERGEANT FARELL
dialogues.text: You might call that notion ironic. But trust me, you'll come around. [clip of Edge of Tomorrow ends]

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Yeah, I mean, and hearing if this was done, like in McCrory style, which is, you know, not improvised, but work as you go. That's just so annoying, because it was so put together. And it's like, how does that happen? When I'm improvising it looks like somebody was improvising. This is nuts! There's so many callbacks, and there's so many lines with double meaning or things that you only realize on the third viewing, they were telling you what's gonna happen, like, like in this in that Bill Paxton speech, from the beginning. It's just, that's just annoying.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: I'm very frustrated by that as well. Because as we talked about before, it's like a recurring thing, where I'm like, why don't they just finish the writing of the movie before they start making the movie? It seems like it would, you'd solve some problems, but apparently, that's not a thing. But like this shows that you just don't need to apparently because like they were still writing the script during editing, they said like, they were making stuff up, like, you know, after they were done with principal photography.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: But like, some people are improv geniuses. And if you're gonna sit them down and give them a script, it's not going to be as great. And you know, clearly these folks making this movie. It worked out, you know?

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: It definitely worked out.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: It's the antithesis, though of Denis. Right? So Denis had the script for 2049. He flew Deakins, and they sat down and for like, two months and storyboarded the entire movie out before they ever started anything. And then his take is once you have everything figured out, then you can throw it away and do whatever you want at the moment, but you always at least have a really solid game plan of where you start from.

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: Yeah, set the bones first, right?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Exactly.

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: That's what you would hope people would do. The interaction between Paxton between Farell and Cage and like that very first interaction them. You know, "I'm not from the United States. I'm from Kentucky." I feel like the whole vibe of that set the movie so well. Because then you're like, oh, wait, this isn't necessarily an action movie. This is a comedy. And that I really appreciate it.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah, going back to like they didn't like the 100 million dollar marketing team did not know what this movie was. If you look at the trailer of this movie, it's billed as like a Tom Cruise action sci fi movie. It's basically Tom Cruise does Independence Day. And like, and it misses the fact that he's a schmuck in this movie, and it misses the fact that it's funny as hell. Like this movie is really funny. I mean, you know, Bill Paxton is doing Foghorn Leghorn. [Lorelei & Haitch laugh] It's like they should put that in the trailer.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Apparently, I didn't find it this time around. But when it came out, and I was just a little bit of research on it, apparently, Tom was very instrumental in that. He really pushed for making it more humorous. And just sort of like influencing that through the way and it's not just the dialogue. It's like tons of like, little like, really humorous tricks and editing and other stuff that was so effective. So why don't you all talk us through the introduction of the armor, the shots of the airfield, the cliffs of Dover? Like what's your take, when you when you see their shots? What do they look like?

dialogues.name: LORELEI I
dialogues.text: want to talk about the armor a little bit.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Please! Go for it.

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: Because, you know, I was watching some of the featurettes and they're talking about, oh, the armor that's not CG. And they kept, I really don't like this whole promotion of, and I know Todd doesn't like this, but the promotion of things is not CG.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: It's all practical!

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: It's all practical! And the thing that really got me was in, in this thing that I watched, it was all about how it's not CGI, as opposed to look how awesome these costumes are. Like it could be a more positive thing where they're really selling that the costumes are just totally amazing. Instead of just repeating over and over again, it's not CG. Because you know what, yeah, there are parts of that costume that are CG. Like missiles coming out of the back, that's not built into that.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: When the thing shoot up and go like that.

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: Yeah, it makes me so sad that people are trying to push down CG. Todd's got something to say I can tell. [Lorelei laughs]

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Oh nooo.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Before you get into, Todd, my question is, this is different though from Green Lantern, right, whatever, Ryan Reynolds where it's a fully CG suit where you know, or Justice League some of the stuff that they did there were to me that just looks, it doesn't look real. And so that's a challenge.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: But like Iron Man. Like you know, like the Iron Man suit in Avengers is also like a painted on suit. And that looks like, I don't know. I mean, that was dope. Like, it's not like that, like loses something because it's -

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Spider Man in every shot in all the new Spider Man movies is 100% CG top to bottom. Same with Black Panther. I mean every single shot.

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: Oh, and the Avengers when they were in their time suits. Like, they hadn't even figured out the the design of those suits at that point, I think. [Lorelei laughs]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Oh my gosh!

dialogues.name: TODD I
dialogues.text: just think it would be weird in a featurette right? Where they're talking about how they build a certain prop. And they're like, oh, no, this isn't foam. This is fiberglass. Right? Foam is just garbage. You know, we want it to be like fiberglass. None of this is real. It's all pretend! You know, Lorelei's heard the speech, you know, it's like, Universal backlot when it's like, oh, there's Joe's diner, you know, they're not selling milkshakes in there. You know, you walk in through that door, here's nothing there. It's plywood all piled up. It's not real. It's all pretend. What does it matter if it tells the story, if it helps the performances. If it guides the film? You know, what does it matter? To say it's one technique or another? It absolutely boggles my mind, it touches on all these things that I think are really interesting. Because if it's done in a computer, it's like, you get it for free or something just like people will spend $700 on a new phone, but an app that might change their life and how they like organize their world for 99 cents? Mmm. Let me think about that. I mean, it's just software! [Haitch laughs] You know, this thing was just created in this little phone here. And this for some reason, that same anything digital means to some people, I don't know that it didn't take craft, or it didn't make there weren't artistic choices involved.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Oh, my God, I definitely don't think that.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: You do Matt! You've said it before! You hate Todd! [Lorelei laughs]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: This is the part where I kick Todd off the show. So Denis famously, and specifically on Dune basically didn't use any green screen, there's I think, like one shot that they had green screen, but he insisted on building, all practical, you know, massive sets for the stuff that they were doing. And from his perspective, it's all about letting the actors be able to capture the moment and have something to react to. So I get it.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: But now they're, I mean, now they're getting around that with doing your stagecraft stuff, right? Like now like they're allowing the actors react to real stuff that is still is computer generated, just they can see it. Like while they're shooting the scene.

dialogues.name: LORELEI I
dialogues.text: feel a lot of this is just being resistant to new technology. And I know CG has been around for a while now. But I've been reading this history about when they first introduced sound, right? Like back in the 20s. They were like, oh, here's sound. And everyone was resistant to it, because they didn't, that's not how movies are told. Movies are told with images and title cards. And it took forever for them to like, convince people that actually sounds pretty good. And I think, I feel like we're in that process right now. Where it's just, people are like ah new technology! I don't like it. Where's my phone puppet?

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: As someone who doesn't have like a actual dog in the fight from, you know, I don't work on movies. Like, what I can't relate to, like professionally, I worked in digital communications in a political sense as my last job. And that world is very much treated by traditional communications as like, where the magic happens, like the cool kids are like doing like their magic tricks, like harnessing the power of computers and the internet, who knows how it works. But like, you know, those of us who've been in the business, like going back to like, you know, the, you know, press secretary under Kennedy, or whatever, this is like the real, this is what real people do in this business. So there's just like, this way in which like, sort of some parts of some industries, the traditional part treats the digital part as like magical and fanciful and like, you know, full of magic tricks. Yeah. And it's like, it's cheating. You get like, you know, you get it for free or something like that.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: It's not real.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah, so I feel your rage on that point.

dialogues.name: TODD I
dialogues.text: just don't, I kind of don't get it. But you know, it's something that is - also when an actor or a stunt performer is on set, and they're working so hard with all these rigs, and these props and doing these stunts and cameras in their face, and they're like, what are you doing here? You're like, well, we're doing these amazing stunts, and we out here are all doing the best we can with these rigs. We're building these elaborate camera systems and cranes and, and the stunt work is just astounding. That's their world! They should be proud of it! It is amazing work! They don't know what's going to go on for the next 8 to 10 months, 12 months on the film's life. And when they're saying that, yes, they're building these tremendous sets for the actors to be able to visualize, to be able to see, perform against and get inspired by. But they don't know what's gonna happen in the next, you know, year in post-production, or what is the plan to have all of that stuff replaced. Or it really didn't work out or it doesn't film well, you know, all the best intentions. Like the suits in Edge of Tomorrow are amazing. And it was really amazing to get the actors in there to to get acquainted with it. All of them do all sorts of training, the movement training. It informed how they performed in those suits.That said, every single time they're like doing anything other than a walk, they're all on a wire being suspended. Whenever anything had to move on the thing. Sometimes it was a prop and sometimes it was all CG. I mean, whatever gets the job done, and makes the thing look as cool and tell the story as well as possible.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: That's it. I'm with you. I'm with you. dude.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: You've convinced him. He was against you before, but now he's with you.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: No, no, no!

dialogues.name: LORELEI
dialogues.text: You're no longer getting kicked off.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: No, Thanos was the, like the turning point for me. Like just just to see like, it's amazing how much further even things have come just over the last couple of years.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah, well, the state of the art also dramatically continued to improve.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah, I love seeing it. Alright, so back to you know, the shots. I just love the shots of Heathrow and all the things taken off and all the soldiers running around there. But now we get to the the actual invasion and so they're in the shuttle. You know, flying and just the ship going completely haywire all the all the bullshit with the Squad and him being just completely razzed and being desperate the whole time to try and figure out how to get out of there. But once you have the shot, where he attempts to drop, and he gets caught on the wires, and he's spinning around, like that shot to me was just absolutely insane! And it went on for a really long time.

dialogues.name: TODD
dialogues.text: Getting all the way up to that point. You don't really realize that this is this movie is almost entirely told from Cage's point of view. And that is a Tom Cruise trait in a lot of his modern films. War of The Worlds and even you know, one of the Mission Impossible movies there. It's mostly told from his point of view, and you're along for the ride and his charisma is taking you along for the ride but that that moment when he does fall and a lot of the audio just kind of drops out after the slow build up of the intensity of the soundtrack and everybody just looking at him and he's just sweating. And he's like, I don't know, as the audience, we don't know what's going going on. And he doesn't know what's going on. And it's so powerful. And when he when he finally does the drop, and it all cuts out. It's just masterful, 'cause you're just like realizing, oh shit! You know! [Lorelei laughs]

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: This is bad.

dialogues.name: TODD
